description:
You are a cloud-architect who must migrate a tiny on-disk SQLite database and prove that the data arrived intact.

    Current layout:
        • /home/user/legacy/old_db.sqlite     ← already present

    What that legacy database contains (for your information only, *do not recreate it*):
        table employees (
            id           INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
            first_name   TEXT,
            last_name    TEXT,
            department   TEXT
        )
        ┌────┬────────────┬───────────┬──────────────┐
        │ id │ first_name │ last_name │  department  │
        ├────┼────────────┼───────────┼──────────────┤
        │  1 │ Ava        │ Moore     │ Engineering  │
        │  2 │ Liam       │ Johnson   │ Finance      │
        │  3 │ Emma       │ Davis     │ Marketing    │
        │  4 │ Noah       │ Wilson    │ HR           │
        │  5 │ Mia        │ Brown     │ Sales        │
        └────┴────────────┴───────────┴──────────────┘

    Task – perform all four steps entirely from the Linux shell:

        1. Create a folder called /home/user/migration.
        2. Migrate every row in table employees from /home/user/legacy/old_db.sqlite
           to a brand-new SQLite database located at /home/user/migration/new_db.sqlite.
           After the operation, both databases must contain exactly the same 5 rows.
        3. Verify the migration by counting the rows in employees in BOTH databases.
        4. Write /home/user/migration/migration_log.txt that captures the result using
           *exactly* this 5-line key=value format (no extra whitespace, no header line):

               SOURCE_DB_PATH=/home/user/legacy/old_db.sqlite
               DEST_DB_PATH=/home/user/migration/new_db.sqlite
               SOURCE_ROW_COUNT=5
               DEST_ROW_COUNT=5
               MIGRATION_STATUS=SUCCESS

           • Row counts must be numeric.
           • MIGRATION_STATUS must be SUCCESS only when the counts match.

    The automated grader will check:
        a. /home/user/migration/new_db.sqlite exists.
        b. The employees table inside the new database has 5 rows that exactly match the source.
        c. /home/user/migration/migration_log.txt is present and its content is byte-for-byte identical to the 5-line template above.

    Hint: you will likely use 2–3 sqlite3 commands along with standard shell tools, but the
    precise commands are your choice. Remember to create the destination directory before
    writing anything into it, and do not modify the legacy database.
truth:
Legacy database path:
        /home/user/legacy/old_db.sqlite

    Legacy database schema and data:
        CREATE TABLE employees (
            id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
            first_name TEXT,
            last_name TEXT,
            department TEXT
        );
        INSERT INTO employees VALUES
            (1,'Ava','Moore','Engineering'),
            (2,'Liam','Johnson','Finance'),
            (3,'Emma','Davis','Marketing'),
            (4,'Noah','Wilson','HR'),
            (5,'Mia','Brown','Sales');

    Expected artefacts created by the agent:

        1. Directory:
            /home/user/migration                     (must exist)

        2. SQLite database:
            /home/user/migration/new_db.sqlite
            • Contains one table 'employees' whose schema matches the source.
            • SELECT COUNT(*) FROM employees;  →  5
            • SELECT * FROM employees ORDER BY id; returns identical 5 rows.

        3. Log file:
            /home/user/migration/migration_log.txt
            Exact content (including newlines, no trailing spaces):

                SOURCE_DB_PATH=/home/user/legacy/old_db.sqlite
                DEST_DB_PATH=/home/user/migration/new_db.sqlite
                SOURCE_ROW_COUNT=5
                DEST_ROW_COUNT=5
                MIGRATION_STATUS=SUCCESS

    The grader will:
        • Verify that /home/user/migration/new_db.sqlite exists.
        • Open both databases and compare SELECT * FROM employees ORDER BY id.
        • Read /home/user/migration/migration_log.txt and compare it literally to the block above.
